port-access secure-update abp

Syntax

port-access secure-update abp

no port-access secure-update abp

Description

Application based policies (ABP) use secure updates by default. When secure updating is enabled, client
traffic affected by ABP modifications is temporarily blocked during the update process. The system installs
override entries in the relevant TCAM table(s) with higher priority than the existing ABP being modified.

Public

port-access secure-update abp 458

These override entries block all traffic for the affected client throughout the update duration, preventing any
unauthorized traffic from passing while the ABP is being changed. Once the update completes, the override
entries are removed and traffic processing resumes using the new ABP rules.

The secure update mechanism also applies to replace operations, where override entries similarly block
traffic while one application based policy is being replaced with another.

When secure update is disabled, no override entries are created during ABP modifications. This approach
provides faster policy updates. However, minor packet loss may occur for permitted traffic during the update
process until the new rules are fully applied.

The no form of the command disables the use of secure updates by default.

Examples

Enabling secure updates:

switch(config)# port-access secure-update abp

Disabling secure updates:

switch(config)# no port-access secure-update abp

show port-access abp

Public

show port-access abp 459

Syntax

show [run] port-access abp

show port-access abp [<POLICY-NAME>]

Description

Displays the application policies and its current usages. If the command is used without specifying the
POLICY-NAME then it displays the details of all the configured application policies on the switch.

Parameter

Application Policy

Description

Types of Application Policy:

Application policy status

The current running status of the Application policy:

•  Local—User configured policy
•  DUR—Downloadable User Role policies

•  Applied—Policy is successfully applied in the hardware
•  Rejected—Policy is not supported in the hardware.
•

In‐Progress—Policy is being processed in the hardware.

Name of the application based policy to associate with the role
(maximum 128 characters).

POLICY‐NAME

Examples

The following example display all application policies configured on the switch:

switch# show port-access abp

Port Access Application Policy User Configured Policy Details:

==============================================================

App Policy Name   : app1

App Policy Type   : Local

App Policy Status : Applied
SEQUENCE    CLASS                            TYPE     ACTION

----------- -------------------------------- --------

-----------------------

10          class1                           abp-ipv4  permit

App Policy Name   : app-policy

App Policy Type   : local

App Policy Status : applied

SEQUENCE    CLASS                         TYPE       ACTION

----------- ----------------------------- ----------

-----------------------------------

10          app-class                     abp-ipv4   drop

Public

show port-access abp 460

20          app-class                     abp-ipv4   dscp AF31

30          app-class                     abp-ipv4   local-priority 5

40          app-class                     abp-ipv4   mirror 4

50          app-class                     abp-ipv4   local-priority 3 dscp

AF11 mirror 4
When no application policies are configured

switch# show port-access abp

Application policy is not configured.
When the specified application policy does not exist

switch# show port-access abp plcy

Application Based Policy does not exist.
Display the policy currently running on the port.

switch# show run port-access abp

port-access abp app1

    10 class abp-ip class1

Usage

The show port-access abp command displays all the active configuration. It providing the list of classes that
are configured and accepted by the system.

The show running-config port-access abp command may not be the same as in active configuration. This is
due to the following:

•  unsupported command parameters

•  class modified after the app policy was applied

•  ABP configuration was unsuccessful due to a lack of hardware resources

Compare the output of the show port-access abp and show running-config port-access abp commands
to see if there is a mismatch between what was configured and what is active. If the active abp and the
configured abp are not the same, a warning message is displayed to help troubleshoot the problem.

If the port-access abp is being processed, an in-progress message will be displayed.

switch(config)# show run

...

! port-access abp <POLICY-NAME> user configuration currently being processed

! run 'show port-access abp' to display active application policy

! configuration.

port-access abp policy_1

...
switch(config)# show running-config port-access abp

! port-access abp <POLICY-NAME> user configuration currently being processed

! run 'show port-access abp' to display active application policy

! configuration.

Public

show port-access abp 461

port-access abp policy_1

10 class abp-ip app_ip_class action drop
If the warning or in-progress message is displayed, additional changes may be made until the error message
is no longer displayed when show port-access abp, show port-access abp commands, show port-access
abp commands configuration, or run the port-access abp reset commands are entered.

The port-access abp reset command changes the user-specified configuration to match the active
configuration.

Display details of a particular application policy that needs reset:

switch(config-pa-abp)# show run port-access abp

! port-access abp plcy user configuration does not match active

configuration.

! run 'port-access abp <POLICY-NAME> reset' to reset application policy to

match

! the active configuration.

port-access abp plcy

    10 class abp-ip cs action drop

    20 class abp-ipv6 cls6

switch(config-pa-abp)# show port-access abp plcy

Port Access Application Policy User Configured Policy Details:

==============================================================

App Policy Name   : plcy

App Policy Type   : Local

App Policy Status : Rejected

SEQUENCE    CLASS                            TYPE     ACTION

----------- -------------------------------- --------

-----------------------

10          cs                               abp-ipv4 drop

20          cls6                             abp-ipv6 permit

switch# port-access abp plcy reset

Following abp entries will be removed:

30 class abp-ip cls2
Do you want to continue (y/n)? y

switch# sh running-config port-access abp

port-access abp plcy

    10 class abp-ip cls action drop

    20 class abp-ipv6 cls6

show port-access abp hitcounts

Syntax

show port-access abp [<POLICY-NAME>] hitcounts

Description

This command is used to show the statistics of the application policy applied on the client. The output helps
to identify the application policy entries that are currently matched.

Parameter

hitcounts

Description

The hit counts (statistics) of the application policy

Public

show port-access abp hitcounts 463

Parameter

POLICY‐NAME

Usage

Description

Name of this application policy

If a class entry is configured with the count action, then the show command will display the statistics of that
entry. The class entries without the count action are not displayed in the hitcounts output. For collecting the
statistics for a specific client, create a copy of the desired policy and attach it to the respective client.

Examples

The following example display show hitcounts of an Application Policy

Troubleshooting

The recommended ARC troubleshooting procedure is provided below:

Step 1: Verify configuration correctness

Run the  show running-config app-recognition  command and verify all the ARC
configurations are accurate.

1.  ip source-lockdown resource-extended  disabled.

2.  app-recognition  enabled globally.

3.  app-recognition  enabled at port level or for the role.

switch# show running-config app-recognition

app-recognition

    enable

interface 1/1/1

    app-recognition enable
If you are using ARC on a user-role instead of a client-port, run the  show port-access clients
command and  show port-access role name <test-arc-role>  to confirm the following:

•  The port is assigned with the proper role

•  app-recognition  enabled for the role

switch# show port-access clients

switch# show port-access role name test-arc-role

Role Information:

Name  : test-arc-role

Type  : local

----------------------------------------------

    Access VLAN                         : 99
    App Recognition                     : enabled

Step 2: Verify no Failure Reason set

There should be no Failure reason. The user-config and Open-Status should be enabled for the desired
ports.

switch# show app-recognition

  Application Recognition Global Configuration
     Configuration status  : Enabled

     Operational status    : Enabled

     Failure Reason        : NA

  Application Recognition Port Configuration

Public

Troubleshooting 480

Interface       User-config       Port-access-config      Oper-status

    -----------     -----------       ------------------      ----------

   1/1/1              Enabled             Disabled            Enabled

   1/1/51             Enabled             Disabled            Enabled

Step 3: Check event logs

show events -r –d arcd —Display the event logs from the current boot.

switch# show events -r -d arcd

---------------------------------------------------

Event logs from current boot

---------------------------------------------------

2023-02-19T23:22:44.819495+08:00 6300 arcd[3541]: Event|14105|LOG_INFO|UKWN|

1|ARCD Publisher is ENABLED

2023-02-19T23:22:44.809134+08:00 6300 arcd[3541]: Event|14101|LOG_INFO|UKWN|

1|App Recognition feature has been ENABLED

Step 4: Verify if the desired flows are created

The  diag-dump flow-tracking basic  command displays the global flow cache. Look under the
ARCD Global FLOW Data  section of the diag-dump.

switch# diagnostics

switch# diag-dump arc basic

Step 5: Gather support-files all and open a TAC case if the problem is unidentified or unresolved

For more information on how to  copy support-files all , see Diagnostics and Supportability
Guide.

IP Flow Information Export

IP Flow Information Export (IPFIX) is an embedded network flow analysis tool that compiles characteristic
and measured properties of flows and sends flow reports to internal or external flow collectors. IPFIX is
configurable via CLI or REST. IPFIX is configurable via the command-line or REST interfaces. With IPFIX,
customers configure flow records with match (key) fields and collection (non-key) fields. Match fields are the
set of fields that define a flow, such as IP address or UDP port. Collection fields are the set of fields that
identify information to collect for a flow, such as packet and byte counters.

A flow exporter defines where and how to export flow reports. Flow exporters are created as standalone
entities in the config context to provide flow monitors the ability to export flow reports. A single flow
exporter can be assigned to one or more flow monitors, and multiple flow exporters can be assigned to a
single flow monitor.

Public

IP Flow Information Export 481

IPFIX is a stand alone feature and can work on its own to monitor the traffic flow. On enabling the
Application Recognition and Control feature, application information along with flow properties are exported
to external or internal IPFIX collectors.

For more information about IPFIX, see the Monitoring Guide .

Traffic Insight

Traffic insight allows monitoring of large amount of data that it collects from various flow exporters like
IPFIX provide the ability to filter, aggregate, and sort the data based on user flow monitor requests. Traffic
insight tracks different monitor requests simultaneously and provides monitor reports per request.

Subtopics

Protocol and feature details
Supported Platforms
Caveats for Traffic Insight
Configuring Traffic Insight
Traffic insight commands

Protocol and feature details

When traffic insight is enabled and clients on the network access SaaS services or public websites, a flow
exporter like IPFIX caches flows in both directions and collects traffic statistics and source and destination
MAC addresses.

The Application Recognition and Control (ARC) feature identifies the application corresponding to these
flows and reports them to IPFIX. The IPFIX sends the flow record to traffic insight, and the traffic insight
feature collects these records and updates the bidirectional traffic information to the Open vSwitch Database
(OVSDB). HPE Aruba Networking Central fetches the flow details from OVSDB through REST APIs.

Supported Platforms

The following table list the supported platforms for Traffic Insight.

Table 1. Supported platforms for Traffic Insight.
Platform

Traffic Insight

5420

Yes

Public

Traffic Insight 482

Platform

6200

6300

6400

Traffic Insight

Yes

Yes

Yes

Supported Monitor Types

Traffic insight supports a variety of monitor types. Refer to this section to learn which monitor types are
supported on each platform.

The 5420, 6200, 6300, 6400, 8100, 8325, 8325H, 8325P, 8360, and 10000 platforms support the
following monitor types:

•  topN-flow monitor

◦  Monitors topN flows by volume (bytes).

◦  By default, the TopN monitor supports 5 entries and a maximum of 20 entries per monitor.

◦  By default IPFIX exports inactive flows every 30 seconds and active flows every 30 minutes.

◦

It is recommended to configure IPFIX active timeout as <=5 minutes when traffic-insight is
configured to monitor topN flows.

◦  New topN flow entries are reported every 5 minutes. You cannot configure the report frequency.

◦  By default, statistics are cleared for every 10 minutes.

The 5420, 6200, 6300, and 6400 platforms support the following monitor types:

•

•

◦  Application Flow Monitor

-

Monitors client application flows to provide application visibility telemetry to HPE Aruba
Networking Central. Telemetry details:

-

-

-

session_count: Number of times client connected to the same application before traffic
insight updates the cache with the client flow details.

src_vlan: Indicates the VLAN in which the packets are received.

policy_action: Flows permissions will vary depending on platform.

-

On the 5420, 6200, 6300, and 6400 Switch Series, only specific flows will be allowed or
blocked by the policy.

Public

Supported Platforms 483

-

On the 6300 and 6400 Switch Series, all flows will be permitted when collect
forwarding-status is not enabled.

Provides client application TX/RX bytes details.

The Traffic Insight application flow table is updated periodically based on the flow cache size. If
the size is above 2k flows, the table is updated once every 30 seconds. If the size is below 2k
flows, the table is updated every 12 minutes. A maximum of 2k flows is updated at a time. Any
excess flows are updated in subsequent updates.

Flow cache limit for 5420 and 6200 Switch Series is 75k.

Flow cache limit for 6300 Switch Series is 75k.

Flow cache limit for 6400 Switch Series is 150k.

The flow update occurs in a first in, first out (FIFO) order based on the flows received from
IPFIX.

AppID flows will vary depending on platform. On the 5420, 6200, 6300, and 6400 Switch Series,
AppID will be updated for all flows if Application Recognition is enabled.

If the flow cache limit is reached, then upcoming new flows are dropped.

-

-

-

-

-

-

-

-

NOTE

Client application flow details are only provided for clients that
connected to edge ports. Therefore, clients must be connected to the
switch through non-infra devices like VOIP phones.

Application flow details are not provided for clients connected to the
switch through network infra devices such as switches or routers.

IPFIX must be enabled on both client-facing and uplink ports.

The Application Recognition feature must be enabled on client-facing
ports.

Short-lived flows blocked by the application-based policy may get
reported as permitted for since few packets are permitted for
identifying the application carried by the flow.

-

policy_type: specifies type of policy/ACL that is blocking the flow. Supported ACL/policy types
for 6200, 6300, and 6400 Switch series:

-

-

-

Global policy: flows dropped/denied by the policy applied on a global scope.

Port ACL: flows dropped/denied by the ACL applied on a port.

Port policy: flows dropped/denied by the policy applied on a port.

Public

Supported Platforms 484

-

-

-

-

-

-

VLAN ACL: flows dropped/denied by the ACL appplied on a VLAN.

VLAN policy: flows dropped/denied by the policy applied on a VLAN.

Interface ACL: flows dropped/denied by the ACL applied on ROP/SVI.

Interface policy: flows dropped/denied by the policy applied on ROP/SVI.

Port access policy: flows dropped/denied by Port Access policy applied on client role. If the
supported ACL/policy types do not block the flow, then this attribute is updated to N/A.

Application based policy: flows dropped/denied by Application Based policy applied on the
client role.

NOTE

If the flow is not blocked due to the ACLs/Policies, then this attribute
is updated as N/A.

-

-

-

policy_name: specifies policy name that is blocking the flow. This value is updated as N/A if the
flow is blocked by a default rule or unsupported ACLs/policies.

class_name: specifies the class name that is matching with the denied flow. This value is updated
as N/A if the flow is blocked by a default rule or unsupported ACLs/policies.

rule_name: flow denied by multiple policies are updated with the sequence number for the class
entry matching the blocked flow. For flow denied by multiple ACLs, the value indicates the
sequence number of the ACL entry matching the blocked flow.

NOTE

Attributes policy_type, policy_name, class_name, rule_name are only
applicable for denied flows. On permitted flows, the attributes will
display N/A.

Policy details are not supported for flows blocked by Group Based Policy
and RACL.

•  Raw Flows Monitor

◦  Monitors uni-direction flow details for all apps or clients to CNX on-demand basis. It is used by CNX

for troubleshooting workflow.

◦  When Central requests data for the flows, the DB is updated from the cache and they will not be

removed from the cache.

◦  Telemetry details

-

src_ip: Source IP address of the flow.

Public

Supported Platforms 485

-

-

-

-

-

-

-

-

-

-

dst_ip: Destination IP address of the flow.

protocol: Type of protocol that is carried by IP.

src_port: Source L4 port of the IP traffic.

dest port: Destination L4 port of the IP traffic.

Bytes: Number of bytes received.

Packets: Number of packets received.

action: Specifies if flow is allowed or blocked due to a policy.

policy_type: Specifies type of policy/ACL that is blocking the flow. Supported ACL/policy types:

-

-

-

-

-

-

-

-

-

Global policy: flows dropped/denied by the policy applied on a global scope.

Port ACL: flows dropped/denied by the ACL applied on a port.

Port policy: flows dropped/denied by the policy applied on a port.

VLAN ACL: flows dropped/denied by the ACL appplied on a VLAN.

VLAN policy: flows dropped/denied by the policy applied on a VLAN.

Interface ACL: flows dropped/denied by the ACL applied on ROP/SVI.

Interface policy: flows dropped/denied by the policy applied on ROP/SVI.

Port access policy: flows dropped/denied by Port Access policy applied on client role. If the
supported ACL/policy types do not block the flow, then this attribute is updated to N/A.

Application based policy: flows dropped/denied by Application Based policy applied on the
client role.

NOTE

If the flow is not blocked due to the ACLs/Policies, then this attribute
is updated as N/A.

policy_name: specifies policy name that is blocking the flow. This value is updated as N/A if the
flow is blocked by a default rule or unsupported ACLs/policies.

class_name: specifies the class name that is matching with the denied flow.This value is updated
as N/A if the flow is blocked by a default rule or unsupported ACLs/policies.

Public

Supported Platforms 486

-

rule_name: flow denied by multiple policies are updated with the sequence number for the class
entry matching the blocked flow. For flow denied by multiple ACLs, the value indicates the
sequence number of the ACL entry matching the blocked flow.

NOTE

Attributes policy_type, policy_name, class_name, rule_name are only
applicable for denied flows. On permitted flows, the attributes will
display N/A.

◦  The recent 5k flows will be pushed to the DB. If flow cache limit of 8k is reached, then the old flow

data will be removed.

NOTE

IPFIX must be enabled on both client-facing and uplink ports.

The 5420, 6300, and 6400 platforms support the following monitor type:

•

◦

◦  DNS Onboarding Latency

-

Monitors DNS request and response flows and provides DNS onboarding latency details per
client.

NOTE

DNS onboarding latency details are published only for clients that
onbaord the switch after enabling the client-insight feature.

This feature is not supported on ROP, LAG and tunnel ports.

This feature is not supported on 6200 Switches.

Flows are merged into the local Merged cache every 30 seconds and are dispatched via MEP. Each MEP
message has a limitation of 8MB, so flows are sent to REST calls or the CLI output in multiple chunks or
pages. The chunk size (number of flows in one message) is dynamic in the case of CLI.

For CNX, the Page size is pre-determined by CNX and honored by TI. CNX can set the maximum number of
flows to be sent with the LIMIT filter.

In case a  show  CLI query is issued, the output is limited to the latest 4k of data only. The  show  CLI
query doesn't delete the flow from the cache, but if the local Merged cache is full, old flows are removed in
FIFO order.

Refer to flow cache size for the maximum number of flows allowed in the Merged cache.

Public

Supported Platforms 487

The 5420, 6300, 6400, and 8360 platforms support the following monitor type:

•  DNS Average Latency

◦  Monitors dns request and response flows and provides average dns-latency details per client

◦  Every 5 minutes the client insights table in the data base is updated with dns average latency details

NOTE

After enabling the client-insight feature the DNS average latency details
are published for clients that onboard the switch.

Caveats for Traffic Insight

The following section provides details on the caveats to be noted for Traffic Insight.

•  Only one traffic-insight instance is allowed.

•  Maximum number of supported traffic insight monitors on the 6300 and 6400 Switch Series is 5.

•  Maximum number of supported traffic insight monitors on the 6200 Switch Series is 5.

•  Maximum number of supported traffic insight monitors on the 5420 Switch Series is 5.

•  Traffic-Insight supports a maximum of 20 entries per TopN monitor.

•  Application and raw flows are maintained locally, but the cache is cleared during ISSU, redundancy

switchover or daemon-restart events.

•  Maximum number of supported raw flow entries is 8k.

•  On 6200 and 6300 Switch Series, the maximum number of supported application flow entries is 75k.

•  On the 6400 Switch Series, the maximum number of supported application flow entries is 150k.

•  The CLI output is limited to the latest 4k of data only.

Configuring Traffic Insight

Prerequisite:

•  Enable Application Recognition and Control (Optional)—If you want to get application information such
as application name, category, and description. Then ARC must be enabled on client-facing ports and
IPFIX on both client-facing and uplink ports for IPFIX to export application details to Traffic Insight.

Public

Caveats for Traffic Insight 488

•  Enable IPFIX—Flow data is exported to Traffic Insight, which is an internal IPFIX collector.

•  Both IPFIX and Client Insight feature should be enabled for Traffic Insight to publish the dns average

latency.

NOTE

The Application Recognition feature is only supported on the 5420, 6200, 6300,
and 6400 Switch Series.

Traffic Insight Configuration

Complete the steps below to configure traffic insight.

1. Create and enable Traffic Insight instance and specify the flow source

switch(config)# traffic-insight TI_Instance-01 -->Creates a Traffic Insight

   instance with name TI_Instance-01

switch(config-ti)# source ipfix

  -->Sets the source protocol to

switch(config-ti)# enable

  -->Enables Traffic Insight

      collect flow information

2. Specify the flow configuration

switch(config)# flow exporter <EXPORTER_NAME>

    destination type traffic-insight

    destination traffic-insight test

    template data timeout <TIMEOUT_VALUE>

switch(config)# flow record <RECORDED_NAME>

    match ipv4 destination address

    match ipv4 protocol

    match ipv4 source address

    match ipv4 version

    match transport destination port
    match transport source port

    collect counter bytes

    collect counter packets

    collect application dns response-code

    collect timestamp absolute first

    collect timestamp absolute last

switch(config)# flow monitor <MONITOR_NAME>

    cache timeout active <TIMEOUT_VALUE>

    exporter <EXPOTER_NAME>

    record <RECORDED_NAME>

switch(config)# interface <Inboud_Interface>

switch(config-if)#ip flow monitor flow-monitor-1 in   -->Enable IPv4 flow

Public

Configuring Traffic Insight 489

monitor

on inbound interfaces

switch(config)# interface <Outbound_Interface>

switch(config-if)#ip flow monitor flow-monitor-1 in

To enable IPv6 flow monitor on inbound interfaces:

switch(config-if)# ipv6 flow monitor flow-monitor-1 in

The 6200 Switch Series is more limited and has the following flow record collect options only:

6200(config)# flow record Record1

6200(config-flow-record)# collect

application  Configure application fields

counter      Configure counter fields

timestamp    Configure timestamp fields

6200(config-flow-record)# match

ip         Configure IPv4 fields

ipv4       Deprecated, please use option 'ip'

ipv6       Configure IPv6 fields

transport  Configure transport fields

6200(config-flow-record)#
The options below are only applicable to the 5420, 6300, and 6400 Switch Series.

IPFIX-6300-VSF(config-flow-record)# match

ip         Configure IPv4 fields

ipv4       Deprecated, please use option 'ip'

ipv6       Configure IPv6 fields

transport  Configure transport fields

IPFIX-6300-VSF(config-flow-record)# collect

application        Configure application fields

counter            Configure counter fields

egress             Specify egress fields

forwarding-status  Forwarding status

timestamp          Configure timestamp fields

IPFIX-6300-VSF(config-flow-record)#

3. Specify the monitoring parameters for flow monitoring

switch(config-ti)# monitor TopN-2-app type topN-flows entries 20 group-by

appid

switch(config-ti)# monitor Monitor_Traffic_On_Interface_ABP type topN-flows

entries 20
switch(config-ti)# monitor TopN-3-srcport type topN-flows entries 20 group-

by appid filter-by srcport 3876

switch(config-ti)# monitor DNSLatency type dns-average-latency

switch(config-ti)# monitor Monitor_Traffic_On_Interface_ARC type

Public

Configuring Traffic Insight 490

application-flows

switch(config-ti)# monitor DNSLatency type dns-average-latency

switch(config-ti)# monitor DNSLatency type dns-onboarding-latency

4. Specify the show commands to get the desired output

switch# show traffic-insight instance-1 monitor-type dns-average-latency

monitor type

Syntax

monitor <MONITOR-NAME> type <FLOW-TYPE>

   entries <NUM>

   running-statistics-timeout <NUM>

   group-by dstip|dstport|ipproto|srcip|srcport|appid|srcip_dstip|

srcip_dstport|srcip_appid|egress_interface|egress_interface_queue
   filter-by {src_ip_mask <string>}|{dst_ip_mask <string>}|{src_ip
<string>}|{dst_ip <string>}|{src_port <string>}|{dst_port <string>}|

{ip_proto <string>}|{egress_interface <string>}|{egress_queue <string>}

Public

monitor type 496

Description

Enables flow monitoring on a traffic insight instance. A traffic insight instance consists of one or more flow
monitor types.

Parameters

Parameter

<MONITOR‐NAME>

<FLOW‐TYPE>

Description

Specifies the name of the monitor. Length: 3 to 80 alphanumeri
c characters, including underscore (_).

Specifies the flow monitor type. See Usage for details on each s
upported flow type.

entries <NUM>

Specifies the number of entries.

running‐statistics‐timeout
<360‐86400>

group‐by

filter‐by

Configure a running‐statistics reset timeout in seconds (Defa
ult: 600 seconds). TopN flow monitor report entries are updated
every 5 minutes and entries are cleared after the running‐stat
istics‐timeout is expired. It is recommended to configure a run
ning‐statistics‐timeout value in the range between 540 and
2700 seconds to avoid entries getting cleared within 1‐2 min
utes once a report is generated.

Groups the monitored data based on the parameters specified.
The parameters are as follows:

ipproto: Group by IP Protocol.

•  dstip: Group by Destination IP.
•  dstport : Group by Destination Port.
•
•  srcip : Group by Source IP.
•  srcport : Group by Source Port.
•  appid : Group by Application ID.
•  srcip_dstip : Group by Source IP and Destination IP.
•  srcip_dstport: Group by Source IP and Destination Port.
•  srcip_appid : Group by Source IP and Application ID.
•  egress_interface : Group by Egress Interface.
•  egress_interface_queue: Group by Egress Interface and QoS

Queue.

Filters by the parameters specified. The parameters are as follo
ws:

Public

monitor type 497

Parameter

Description

•  dst_ip <string>: Filter by Destination IP.
•  dst_ip_mask <string>: Filter by Destination IP Mask.
•  dst_port <string>: Filter by Destination Port.
•

ip_proto <string>: Filter by IP Protocol. Supported options a
re tcp, udp, icmp, ipv6‐icmp
•  src_ip <string>: Filter by Source IP.
•  src_ip_mask <string>: Filter by Source IP Mask.
•  src_port <string>: Filter by Source Port.
•  egress_interface <string>: Filter by Egress Interface.
•  egress_queue <string>: Filter by Egress Queue.

Usage

The following flow monitor types are supported:

•  topN-flows: Monitors IP traffic flowing through the switch and captures topN volume flows. The default
number of top flows captured is 5. Running statistics are maintained for topN flows. By default running
statistics are cleared for every 600 seconds. This flow monitor type is supported on 5420, 6200, 600,
6400, 8100, 8325, 8325H, 8325P, 8360, 9300, 9300S and 10000 Switch series.

◦  A topN flow monitor caches flows up to 10 minutes to show total flow volume for last 10 minutes.

The cache size will grow if there are many short-lived flows in the network. Do not set the
running-statistics-timeout value to less than the default value (10 minutes) when there are many
short lived flows in the network.

◦  Memory usage can be high if multiple topN monitors are configured, so best practices to create no

more than two topN monitors.

◦

It is not recommended to configure a topN monitor when application-flow or dns-average-latency
monitors are configured, and vice-versa.

•  application-flows: Monitors client unicast application flows and provides application level rx/tx
counters, and also brings application visibility. This is supported on 5420, 6200, 6300 and 6400
platforms. It is also supported in 6200 with limitations with regards to Application Visibility because of
the absence of Application Recognition feature.

•  raw-flows: This flow monitor provides the following telemetry details:

◦  src_ip : Source IP address of the flow.

◦  dst_ip : Destination IP address of the flow.

◦  protocol : Type of protocol that is carried by IP.

◦  src_port : Source L4 port of the IP traffic

Public

monitor type 498

◦  dest port : Destination L4 port of the IP traffic.

◦  app_details : Application details.

◦  Bytes : Number of bytes received.

◦  Packets : Number of packets received.

◦  action : Specifies if flow is allowed or blocked due to a policy.

◦  egrq : Specifies Egress Queue of the flow.

◦  egrif : Specifies Egress Physical Interface of the flow.

◦

igrif : Specifies Ingress Physical Interface of the flow.

If the flow cache limit is reached, then the old flow data is removed. This feature is supported on 5420,
6200 and 6300 and 6400 platforms.

•  dns-average latency: Monitors DNS request and response flows and provides average DNS latency

details per client. The client insights table in the DB is updated with DNS average latency details every
5 minutes. This is supported in 5420, 6300, 6400 and 8360 platforms. In 5420, 6300 and 6400
platforms, it monitors DNS failures and populates DNS failure reason codes.

•  dns-onboarding-latency: Monitors DNS request and response flows and provides onboarding DNS

latency details per client. This is supported in 5420, 6300 and 6400 platforms.

•  dropped-flows: Monitors traffic flows that are dropped. If there are any dropped flows, the flow

monitor shows the corresponding drop reasons sorted into groups, like L1, L2, L3 and MCAST. The
Traffic-Insight Dropped-Flow table is updated once every 12 minutes, unless the flow count exceeds
1000 flows, at which point the table is updated every 30 seconds). A maximum of 1000 flows can be
written to the database in a single update. This feature is supported on the 5420, 6200, 6300, 6400,
8100, 8325, 8325H, 8325P, 8360, and 9300 platforms.

•  congested-flows: The Congested Flows feature monitors network traffic flows that become congested
when egress queues exceed their configured thresholds. When congestion is detected, the system logs
the associated egress interface, queue, threshold, and the timestamp of the event. The Traffic Insight
Congested Flow table is refreshed every 12 minutes under normal conditions. However, if the number of
congested flows exceeds 1,000, the update frequency increases to every 30 seconds. Each update can
record up to 1,000 flows in the database. This feature is supported on the following platforms: 5420,
6200, 6300, 6400, 8100 and 8360.

Examples

The following example configures mntr1 with topN-flows:

switch(config-ti)# monitor mntr1 type topN-flows running-statistics-timeout

1800

Public

monitor type 499

switch(config-ti)# monitor mntr1 type topN-flows filter-by ipproto tcp

switch(config-ti)# monitor mntr2 type topN-flows group-by src_ip filter-by

src_ip

mask 192.0.0.0/8

switch(config-ti)# monitor mntr1 type topN-flows entries 10
The following example configures mntr3 with application-flows:

switch(config-ti)# monitor mntr3 type application-flows

The following example configures mntr3 with raw-flows:

switch(config-ti)# monitor mntr3 type raw-flows

Command History

show debug buffer module trafficinsight

Syntax

show debug buffer module trafficinsight

Description

Displays Traffic Insight debug logs stored in the debug buffer.

Examples

Switch# show debug buffer module trafficinsight

-----

show debug buffer

------

show events traffic-insightd

Syntax

show events -d traffic-insightd

Description

Displays event logs generated by the switch modules since the last reboot for Traffic Insight.

Examples

Showing event logs of Traffic Insight:

Public

show events traffic-insightd 505

Switch# show events -d traffic-insightd

---------------------------------------------------

Event logs from current boot

---------------------------------------------------

2022-10-26T07:55:17.369208+00:00 6410 traffic-insightd[2518]: Event|14005|

LOG_INFO|UMM|-|Traffic Insight instance t1 enabled

2022-10-26T07:55:17.369309+00:00 6410 traffic-insightd[2518]: Event|14001|

LOG_INFO|UMM|-|Instance t1 created

show running-config traffic-insight

Syntax

show running-config traffic-insight

Description

Display configuration settings for all traffic insight instances.

Public

show running-config traffic-insight 506

Examples

Example show running-config traffic-insight output for 5420 ,6200, 6300, 6400, 8100, 8325, 8325P,
8325H, 8360, 9300, 9300S and 10000 Switch series:

switch(config-ti)# monitor mntr1 type topN-flows entries 20 group-by

srcip_dstip

switch# show running-config traffic-insight

traffic-insight ti

enable

source ipfix

!

monitor top-1 type topN-flows entries 20 group-by srcip_dstip

...

 switch# show running-config traffic-insight

traffic-insight t1

enable

source ipfix

!

monitor mntr1 workload-flows

monitor top-5-src-ip-filter group-by src_ip filter-by src_ip_mask

192.0.0.0/8

show tech traffic-insight

Syntax

show tech traffic-insight

Description

Shows the Traffic Insight configuration settings as displayed in the output of the show running-config
traffic-insight command..

Examples

The following example shows possible output for the show tech traffic-insight command. The output of this
command will vary, depending upon the traffic insight feature options available for each switch model and
the traffic insight configuration.

Switch# show tech traffic-insight

====================================================

Show Tech executed on Wed Oct 26 11:11:37 2022

====================================================

====================================================

[Begin] Feature traffic-insight

====================================================

*********************************

Command : show running-config traffic-insight

*********************************

traffic-insight t1

enable

Public

show tech traffic-insight 508

source ipfix

!

monitor top4 type topN-flows entries 20 group-by srcip

monitor dns type dns-average-latency

====================================================

[End] Feature traffic-insight

====================================================

====================================================

Show Tech commands executed successfully

====================================================

show traffic-insight monitor-type

Syntax

show traffic-insight <INSTANCE_NAME> monitor-type

  (congested-flows) [<MONITOR_NAME> | all]

Public

show traffic-insight monitor-type 509

application-flows <MONITOR_NAME> {app-details|client-role|url-details|

permitted|denied

   application-flows <MONITOR_NAME> {tls-visibility [{client client-mac>}]|

{tls-cert-visibility [detail|expired]

   dns-average-latency <MONITOR_NAME>

   dns-average-latency <MONITOR_NAME> error-statistics

   dropped-flows <MONITOR_NAME>

            topN-flows <MONITOR_NAME>}all

            [app-details]

   raw-flows <MONITOR_NAME>

Description

Display information for traffic insight monitored flows.

NOTE

The start and end times for calculating DNS average latency are always displayed
in UTC time format irrespective of system time zone.

Parameter

Description

<INSTANCE_NAME>

Name of the traffic insight instance, string of maximum length u
p to 32 characters.

application flows

Display client application flow information collected by the spec
ified application‐flows type traffic monitor.

   app‐details

Displays traffic insight monitor flows with application details.

This option is supported only on monitors with one of the follo
wing configurations:

   monitor <MONITOR_NAME> monitor‐type
topN‐flows {filter‐by <FILTER‐TYPE>}
   monitor <MONITOR_NAME> monitor‐type
topN‐flows group‐by appid {filter‐by
<FILTER‐TYPE>}
   monitor <MONITOR_NAME> monitor‐type
topN‐flows group‐by srcip_appid {filter‐by
<FILTER‐TYPE>}
   monitor <MONITOR_NAME> monitor‐type
application‐flows

Public

show traffic-insight monitor-type 510

Parameter

Description

   client‐role

   url‐details

   permitted

   denied

   tls‐visibility [client
  <CLIENT_MAC>]

   tls‐cert‐visibility

detail

expired

   forwarded

   dropped

   interface‐queue

Show traffic insight monitor flows with client role details. This p
arameter is available on 5420, 6200, 6300, and 6400 Switch se
ries.

Show flows with app URL details. This parameter is available on
5420, 6200, 6300, and 6400 Switch series.

Show denied flows for this application‐flows monitor. This pa
rameter is available on 5420, 6200, 6300, and 6400 Switch seri
es.

Show denied flows for this application‐flows monitor. This pa
rameter is available on 5420, 6200, 6300, and 6400 Switch seri
es.

Show TLS attributes for traffic insight flows. Include the option
al client <CLIENT_MAC> parameter with a MAC address in xx:x
x:xx:xx:xx:xx format to display details for a specific client only.
This parameter is available on 5420, 6300 and 6400 Switch ser
ies only.

Show TLS certificate attributes for traffic insight flows. This pa
rameter is available on 5420, 6300 and 6400 Switch series onl
y.

(Optional) Show traffic insight flows with application TLS Certifi
cate Visibility with additional details. This parameter is available
on 5420, 6300 and 6400 Switch series only.

(Optional) Show traffic insight flows with application TLS for ex
pired certificates. This parameter is available on 5420, 6300 an
d 6400 Switch series only.

Show only forwarded flows. This parameter is available on 5420
, 6300 and 6400 Switch series only.

Show only dropped flows. This parameter is available on 5420,
6300 and 6400 Switch series only.

Show traffic insight flows with interface and queue details for
permitted flows. This parameter is available on 5420, 6300 and
6400 Switch series only.

dns‐average‐latency
<MONITOR_NAME>

Show average latency for client DNS flows collected by the spe
cified dns‐average‐latency type traffic monitor. This param
eter is available on 5420, 6300, 6400 and 8360 Switch series.

   error‐statistics

Show DNS flows with error statistics.

Public

show traffic-insight monitor-type 511

Parameter

dropped‐flows

Description

Display dropped flows information collected by a dropped‐flo
ws type traffic monitor.

   <MONITOR_NAME>|all

Specifies traffic insight monitor name. Choose all to display info
rmation for all monitors.

topN‐flows

Display information collected by a topN‐flows type traffic mo
nitor.

[app‐details]

raw‐flows

[Optional] Display additional application details for topN flows.
This optional parameter is available on 5420, 6300, 6400 and
8325 Switch series.

Shows the last 5k flows that switch has received across all the
ports where IPFIX is enabled in non‐chronological order. This
parameter is available on 5420, 6300, 6400 and 8325 Switch s
eries.

congested‐flows

Monitors congested flows.

NOTE

To allow application flows to indicate denied flows appropriately, the IPFIX flow
monitor configuration should include forwarding status and egress VLAN collect
options.

flow

Examples

The following example shows monitoring flow data for mon1, for instance 1, with tcp-conn-summary details:

switch# show traffic-insight instance-1 monitor-type application-flows mon1

app-details tcp-conn-summary

Type      : application-flows

----------------------------------------------------------------------------

-------------

src_ip                         : 192.168.1.6

dst_ip                         : 143.193.61.233

dst_port                       : 26341

app_name                       : whatsapp

tcp_conn_est_min_time_stosw    : 200 us  tcp_conn_est_min_time_ctosw    :

150 us

tcp_conn_est_max_time_stosw    : 300 us  tcp_conn_est_max_time_ctosw    :

200 us

tcp_conn_est_avg_time_stosw    : 250 us  tcp_conn_est_avg_time_ctosw    :

180 us

tcp_conn_est_median_time_stosw : 240 us  tcp_conn_est_median_time_ctosw :

170 us

----------------------------------------------------------------------------
-------------

src_ip                         : 192.168.1.8

dst_ip                         : 143.193.61.235

dst_port                       : 62413

app_name                       : google

tcp_conn_est_min_time_stosw    : 400 us  tcp_conn_est_min_time_ctosw    :

350 us

tcp_conn_est_max_time_stosw    : 600 us  tcp_conn_est_max_time_ctosw    :

500 us

280 us

The following example shows congested traffic when egress queues exceed their configured thresholds, on
5420, 6200, 6300, 6400, 8325, 8325P, 8325H and 9300-32D Switch series:

switch# show traffic-insight test monitor-type congested-flows mon

Name  : mon

Type  : congested-flows

----------------------------------------------------------------------------

---------------

src_ip      : 10.10.10.1                 dst_ip      : 10.10.10.2

src_port    : 1024                       dst_port    : 22

src_vlan    : DEFAULT_VLAN_1             protocol    : 6

igrif       : 1/1/1                      VRF         : default

egrif       : 1/1/2                      egrq        : 4(80%)

Last Observed Congestion Events:

  05/30/2024 21:06:05.400

  05/30/2024 21:06:05.100

  05/30/2024 21:06:04.369
The following example shows an error message displayed when monitor type is incorrect:

switch# show traffic-insight test monitor-type congested-flows mon1 all

Monitor mon1 type is not configured as congested-flows.
The following example shows an error message displayed when monitor name is incorrect:

switch# show traffic-insight test monitor-type congested-flows mon1 all

Monitor mon1 is not configured in traffic insight instance test.

Command History

Release

10.17

Modification

The dropped‐flows flow monitor type is available on 5420. 6200. 8100. 836
0 Switch series.

Introduces congested‐flows monitor type.

10.16.1000

h series.

10.16

10.15

10.13

Command introduced on the 5420 switch series.

enied, client‐role, and url‐details were introduced.

show traffic-insight monitor-type 536

traffic-insight

Syntax

traffic-insight <INSTANCE_NAME>

   enable

   monitor <NAME> type

application-flows

dns-average-latency

dns-onboarding-latency

dropped-flows

error-statistics

raw-flows

topN-flows

entries <Entry-VALUE>

running-statistics-timeout <TIMEOUT-SECONDS>

group-by <GROUP-TYPE>

filter-by <FILTER-TYPE>

   no ...

   source ipfix

Description

Traffic insight monitors data collected from flow exporters like the IP Flow Information Export (IPFIX) flow
exporter. Traffic insight tracks multiple monitor requests simultaneously and provides monitor reports for
each request.

NOTE

An egress Interface can be determined only after MAC and ARP resolution; the
egress interface is exported only after next active/inactive timeout after the MAC
entry is programmed. This is not applicable for 6200 Switch Series.

Parameter

Description

<INSTANCE_NAME>

enable

Name of the traffic insight instance, string of maximum length u
p to 32 characters.

Use the enable parameter to enable this traffic insight configur
ation, or use the no enable option to disable traffic insight.

monitor <INSTANCE_NAME>
type

Enable flow monitoring on a traffic insight instance and configu
re rules for filtering and grouping traffic flows.

   application‐flows

Monitors client application flows and provides application level
rx/tx counters and application visibility. The frequency at which

Public

traffic-insight 537

Parameter

Description

the Traffic Insight application flow table is updated with new flo
w statistics is 30 seconds if the flow count is more than 2k. Oth
erwise, it is updated every 12 minutes. A maximum of 2k flows
can be updated in the table at a time. Any excess flows are upd
ated in subsequent updates.

Application flow record the following telemetry information:

•  client_role—Role assigned to the client while accessing the

application

•  session_count—Number of aggregated sessions
•  source_port—Since multiple sessions aggregated into a sin
gle flow, the source port corresponds to the one of the sessi
on.

•  url_preifx—String indicating the URL accessed.
•  Tx—Tx bytes. No of bytes transmitted from a client to a ser

ver.

•  Rx—Rx bytes. No of bytes client received from a server.
•  policy_action— Indicates whether the flow is Permitted or

Denied by various ACL/Policies.

•  egrq_ctos—Indicates egress queue associated with the flo
w from client to server. This field is tracked on per flow basi
s.

•  egrif_ctos—Indicates egress interface associated with the f
low from client to server. This field is tracked on per flow b
asis.
igrif_ctos—Indicates ingress interface associated with the f
low from client to server. This field is tracked on per flow b
asis.

•

•  egrq_stoc—Indicates egress queue associated with the flo
w from server to client. This field is tracked on per flow basi
s.

•  egrif_stoc—Indicates egress interface associated with the f
low 85 from server to client. This field is tracked on per flo
w basis.
igrif_stoc—Indicates ingress interface associated with the f
low from server to client. This field is tracked on per flow b
asis.

•

For 5420 and 6200 Switch Series, application flows also shows
the following telemetry details:

Public

traffic-insight 538

Parameter

Description

   dns‐average‐latency

•  app_details—Shows traffic insight flows with application d

etails.

•  client_role—Shows role assigned to the client.
•  url_details—Shows traffic insight flows with application UR

L details.

Monitors DNS request and response flows and provides averag
e dns‐latency details per client. The Traffic Insight application f
low table in the database is updated every 5 minutes with dns
average latency information.

   dns‐onboarding‐latency

Monitors DNS request and response flows and provides DNS on
boarding latency details per client.

   dropped‐flows

   raw‐flows

Monitors dropped traffic flows and sorts the corresponding dro
p reasons into the groups such as e L1, L2, L3 and MCAST. The
Traffic‐Insight Dropped‐Flow table is updated once every 1
2 minutes, unless the flow count exceeds 1000 flows, at which
point the table is updated every 30 seconds. A maximum of 10
00 flows can be written to the database in a single update.

This option provides uni‐direction flow details for all applicati
ons or clients to HPE Aruba Networking Central on an on‐de
mand basis. It is used by Central for troubleshooting workflows.

Raw‐flow provides the following telemetry details:

•  src_ip: Source IP address of the flow.
•  dst_ip: Destination IP address of the flow.
•  protocol: Type of protocol carried by IP.
•  src_port: Source L4 port of the IP traffic
•  dest port: Destination L4 port of the IP traffic.
•  app_details: Application details like app_name, category, an

d URL.

•  Bytes: Number of bytes received.
•  Packets: Number of packets received.
•  action: Specifies if flow is allowed or blocked due to a polic

y.

•  egrq: The egress queue of the flow. Not applicable if this va
lue is not received or if an invalid value is received from IPFI
X.

•  egrif: The egress physical Interface of the flow. Not applica
ble if this value is not received or an invalid value is receive
d from IPFIX.
igrif:The ingress physical interface of the flow.

•

Public

traffic-insight 539

Parameter

   topN‐flows

Description

Monitors IP traffic flowing through the switch and captures top
N flows volume. The default number of top flows captured is 5.
This monitor type is available on all switches that support the tr
affic insight feature.

entries <Entry‐VALUE>

Select the maximum number of entries in the monitor report. Ra
nge: 1 to 20. The default value is 5.

running‐statistics‐timeout
<TIMEOUT‐SECONDS>

Sets running‐statistics reset timeout value. Range: 360 to 86
400. The default value is 600.

filter‐by <FILTER‐TYPE>

Include any of the following values to filter the data set.

group‐by <GROUP‐TYPE>

•  src_ip_mask <IP_MASK>—Filter by source IP netmask
•  dst_ip_mask <IP_MASK>—Filter by destination IP netmask
•  src_ip <IP_ADDRESS>—Filter by source IP address
•  dst_ip <IP_ADDRESS>— Filter by destination IP address
•  src_port <PORT>—Filter by source port number
•  dst_port <PORT>:—Filter by destination port number
•
•  appid <string>—Filter by application ID

ip_proto <PROTOCOL>—Filter by IP protocol

(For 5420, 6300, and 6400 Switch series)

•  egress_interface <interface>—Filter by egress interface
•  egress_queue <string>—Filter by egress queue

Include any of the following values to create a monitor that gro
ups matching traffic flows by that criteria.

•  srcip—Group by source IP address
•  dstip —Group by destination IP address
•  srcport—Group by source port number
•  dstport—Group by destination port number
•
ipproto—Group by IP protocol
•  appid—Group by application ID
•  srcip_dstip—Group by source IP and destination IP
•  srcip_dstport—Group by source IP and destination Port
•  srcip_appid—Group by source IP and application ID.

(For 5420, 6300, and 6400 Switch series)

•  egress_interface —Group by egress Interface.
•  egress_interface_queue —Group by egress Interface and

QoS queue.

Public

traffic-insight 540

Parameter

no ...

source ipfix

Examples

Description

Negate a command or reset it to default values.

The traffic insight configuration uses this source protocol to coll
ect traffic flows. The only available protocol is ipfix.

The following example creates a traffic insight instance named TI_1:

switch(config)# traffic-insight  TI_1

The following example deletes a traffic insight instance named TI_1:

switch(config)# no traffic-insight TI_1

The following example enables traffic insight instance for TI_1 instance:

switch(config)# traffic-insight  TI_1

switch(config-ti)#enable

The following example disables traffic insight instance for TI_1 instance:

switch(config)# traffic-insight  TI_1

switch(config-ti)#no enable

The following example sets the source protocol for TI_1 instance to collect flows information from IPFIX:

switch(config)# traffic-insight TI_1

switch(config-ti)# source ipfix

The following example removed the source protocol for TI_1 instance:

switch(config)# traffic-insight TI_1

switch(config-ti)# no source ipfix

The following examples create traffic insight monitor with filter and grouping rules for topN-flows for the
mnti1 monitoring:

switch(config)# traffic-insight TI_1

switch(config-ti)# monitor mnti1 type topN-flows

switch(config-ti)# monitor mnti1 type topN-flows running-statistics-timeout

1800
switch(config-ti)# monitor mntil type topN-flows group-by srcip

switch(config-ti)# monitor mntil type topN-flows filter-by src_ip_mask

192.0.0.0/8

Public

traffic-insight 541

The following example creates a traffic insight monitor with filter and grouping rules for topN-flows using
the below parameter for the mnti1 monitoring:

•  10 entries

•  Grouped by srcip (Source IP)

•  Filter by src_ip_mask (Source IP Mask)

switch(config-ti)# monitor mnti1 type topN-flows entries 10 group-by srcip

filter-by src_ip_mask 192.0.0.0/8

The following example removes flow monitoring:

switch(config)#traffic-insight TI_1

switch(config-ti)# no monitor mnti1 type topN-flows

The following example creates a traffic insight monitor for application-flows for the mnti2 instance on
5420, 6200, 6300, or 6400 Switch series:

switch(config-ti)# monitor mnti2 type application-flows

The following example creates a traffic insight monitor for raw-flows for the mnti3 instance:

switch(config-ti)# monitor mnti3 type raw-flows

The following examples creates a traffic insight monitor for dns-average-latency for the mnti3 instance: on
a 5420, 6300, 6400, or 8360 Switch series

switch(config-ti)# monitor mnti3 type dns-average-latency

switch(config-ti)# monitor mntr1 type workload-flows
The following example creates a traffic insight monitor for dns-onboarding-latency for the mnti3 instance
on a 5420, 6300, or 6400 Switch series:

switch(config-ti)# monitor mnti3 type dns-onboarding-latency

The following example creates a traffic insight monitor <NAME> type for topN flows on a 5420, 6300,
6400, 8100 or 8360 Switch series:

switch(config)# monitor <NAME> type (topN-flows)

[entries <NUM> | running-statistics-timeout <NUM> |

group-by (srcip|dstip|srcport|dstport|ipproto|appid|srcip_dstip|

srcip_dstport|srcip_appid) |

filter-by (src_ip_mask STR | dst_ip_mask STR | src_ip STR |
dst_ip STR | src_port STR |dst_port STR |ip_proto STR |appid STR

)

switch(config)# monitor <NAME> type workload-flows

Traffic Inspection

The traffic inspection feature is designed to inspect client-related traffic for malicious signatures on the
switch. Any detected malicious signatures will be reported; however, the switch will not drop the associated
packets.

Subtopics

Protocol and feature details
Supported Platforms
Caveats for Traffic Inspection
Traffic Inspection commands

Protocol and feature details

The traffic inspection feature utilizes a high-performance network threat detection tool. It analyzes network
traffic and generates real-time alerts for suspicious activities. By deploying this tool, the system captures
and examines packets, identifying anomalies and threats based on predefined rules. This feature leverages
the IDS engine to enable efficient traffic monitoring, offering valuable insights into suspicious activities and
enhancing the network's security posture.

NOTE
The command show port-access client detail can also be used to view the
client-specific Traffic Inspection details.

Public

Traffic Inspection 544

Prerequisites:

•  Set to v2-default profile mode (applicable only for AOS-CX 6400 Switch)

In a 6400 AOS-CX switch, the traffic inspection feature is supported only on V2 (R0X38C, R0X40C,
R0X41C, R0X42C, R0X43C, R0X44C and R0X45C) line cards. To configure  v2-default  profile
mode, execute the command followed by configuration save and reboot command.

To configure v2-default profile, use the following command:

switch(config)# profile v2-default

For more information, see AOS-CX Fundamentals Guide.

•  Disable the IPLD extended mode

IP lockdown resource extended must be disabled to run traffic inspection. To disable the IPLD extended
for AOS-CX switch, use the following command:

switch(config)# no ip source-lockdown resource-extended

NOTE

Please note that IPLD disablement is not applicable to the 5420 Switch Series.

For more information, see AOS-CX IP Services Guide.

Requisites:

•  Flow tracking should be enabled on the system.

•  Traffic inspection must be enabled on the role associated for the port-access clients for the feature to be

operational.

Considerations:

•  Client-Specific TLS Traffic Inspection: Ensure the system inspects TLS traffic for designated clients

based on their assigned roles.

•  User-Friendly Alert Translation: Translate alerts generated by the IDS engine into a format that is easily

understandable for users.

•  Extensible Design for Future Traffic Classes: Design the system with extensibility in mind, allowing for

the inspection of additional types of traffic classes in the future.

Public

Protocol and feature details 545

Supported Platforms

The following table list the supported platforms for Traffic Inspection.

Table 1. Supported platforms for Traffic Inspection.
Platform

Traffic Inspection

5420

6200

6300

6400

Yes

No

Yes

Yes

Caveats for Traffic Inspection

The following section provides details on the caveats to be noted for Traffic Inspection.

•  For TLS sessions requiring more than 14 packets to complete the handshake, the inspection status

becomes indeterminate.

•  Traffic inspection requires an advanced feature-pack to be installed on the system to be operational.

•  Although traffic inspection is supported when the application-recognition is configured as 'fast mode', it

is not recommended.

Traffic Inspection commands

Select a command from the list in the left navigation menu.

Subtopics

traffic-inspection
enable
inspect tls
show traffic-inspection
show traffic-inspection threat-signatures

Public

Supported Platforms 546

traffic-inspection

Syntax

traffic-inspection

no traffic-inspection

Description

Enters the traffic inspection context. The traffic inspection feature is designed to inspect client-related traffic
for malicious signatures on the switch. Any malicious signatures detected will be reported; however, the
switch will not drop the associated packets.

The no form of the command deletes all configurations associated with traffic inspection.

Examples

Enter traffic inspection context:

switch(config)# traffic-inspection

switch(config-traffic-inspection)#

Command History

Release

10.17

Modification

10.16.1000

Command introduced for the 6300 and 6400 Switch Series.

Platforms

Command context

Authority

config

Administrators or local user group members with execution righ
ts for this command.

5420

6300

6400

enable

Public

traffic-inspection 547

Syntax

enable

no enable

Description

This command enables traffic inspection for clients. Additionally, traffic inspection must be enabled on the
role associated with the client for the feature to function properly. Note that traffic inspection requires flow
tracking to be enabled on the system.

The no form of the command disables the traffic inspection.

Examples

Enabling traffic inspection:

switch(config)# traffic-inspection

switch(config-traffic-inspection)# enable

switch(config-traffic-inspection)# enable

Flow tracking is not enabled. Traffic inspection will not be operational

until

flow tracking is enabled.

Continue (y/n)?

switch(config-traffic-inspection)# enable

Application recognition is enabled in fast mode. Traffic inspection may

not receive all the packets of a TLS handshake to identify threats if

application recognition is enabled on the port.

Continue (y/n)?
Disabling traffic inspection:

switch(config)# traffic-inspection

switch(config-traffic-inspection)# no enable

Client Insight

The client insight feature captures L2, L3, and L4 onboarding details such as time taken for authentication,
acquiring IP address and DNS resolution of the clients. The details published by the client insight
feature help in providing better insight into the client activities, using the HPE Aruba Networking Central
application. Client insight can also be configured to generate event logs with client onboarding status.

Figure 1. Onboarding Event Workflow

Public

Client Insight 554

Latency or authentication failures can occur during different stages of L2, L3, or L4 onboarding. The client
onboarding details such as time taken for authentication, acquiring IP address and DNS resolution will be
available as consolidated data for Central consumption, as well as ARP to Gateway latency of ARP Request/
Response during onboard period for each gateway per client

Client insight can be configured to generate event logs with client onboarding status. In the event of failure,
the reason code for the failure is also captured.

The following list describes the different stages of this feature.

•  L2 stage: The client undergoes authentication or authorization. If security is disabled on the port, then
the client is treated as L2 onboarded client. For L2 clients undergoing authentication, RADIUS and
authentication latencies are published in the client insight table. Using the authentication start and end
time, port access publishes authentication start timestamp, end timestamp and authentication latencies
to the client insight table. It also publishes time taken by authentication methods to send request and
receive response from the RADIUS server. From these values, the following authentication latency can be
derived:

◦  dot1x_radius_latency—Time taken to complete 802.1x RADIUS authentication.

◦  macauth_radius_latency—Time taken to complete mac-auth RADIUS authentication.

NOTE

radius_latency values are a subset of auth_latency. Depending on the
configuration, only one of either 802.1X and mac-auth RADIUS latency
values is published. In case of concurrent onboarding or authentication
precedence with priority, when both the methods are enabled, both
latencies are published.

•

Public

Client Insight 555

•  L3 stage: The client acquires an IP address through the DHCP process, in which case the onboarding

latency would be the time taken by the DHCP exchange. Onboarding details are captured only for those
clients that onboard the switch after enabling a client insight configuration. Onboarding data will not be
available or published for the existing clients onboarded before the client insight feature is enabled.
The DHCP discover, offer, request, and acknowledge (DORA) message exchange between the client
and server must be tracked to calculate per-client DHCP latency.

•  L4 stage: The client sends ARP requests to the Gateway and accesses DNS services for DNS name

resolution.

Latency Calculation Details

•  For L2 clients undergoing authentication, multiple types of latency data are published in the

Client_Insight table:

◦  auth start & end time: Port-access publishes authentication start and end timestamps to client

insight tables. Authentication latency can be derived from these values

◦  dot1x_radius_latency: Time taken to complete 802.1x RADIUS authentication.

◦  macauth_radius_latency : Time taken to complete macauth RADIUS authentication. RADIUS latency
values are a subset of authentication_latency. Either 802.1x or mac-auth radius_latency values can
be published, depending on the configuration.

•  For DHCP clients, L3 onboarding latency is the time taken by the clients to acquire an IP address from
the DHCP server. L3 onboarding latency is calculated based on DHCP message exchange start and end
timestamps between client and server. The start and end timestamps are published in DB.

•  The ARP latency is calculated for ARP requests to Gateway only and not any other IP address. If the

DHCP Server is not configured with default gateway settings, the ARP on-boarding status will appear to
have failed in all onboarding events.

•  DNS onboarding latency is calculated based on the first DNS request and response between the client
and the DNS server. The starting and the ending timestamps for each DNS server are published in the
database.

•  Onboarding details are captured for only those clients that onboard to the switch after enabling

client-insight configurations. Onboarding data will not be available or published for existing clients
onboarded before you enable the client-insight feature.

•  Onboarding details will not be captured for clients onboarded during a daemon restart.

•  L3 onboarding starting and ending timestamps are reset to zero after the following events:

◦  Client MAC ages out.

◦  DHCP snooping daemon (ipsavd) restarts.

Public

Client Insight 556

◦  Client sends a DHCP renewal request.

•  Upon successful L3 onboarding, if switch re-learns the client's MAC after the MAC ages out, L3

onboarding timestamp values would be older than L2 onboarding timestamps.

L2 Onboarding Status and Failure Reason Codes

Onboarding status types include:

•  successful : L2 onboarding is successful

•  partial : Authentication is not completely successful. Possible reasons could be because the client is

placed in unintended role such as Fallback or Critical (For example, if RADIUS server is not reachable),
or the L2 onboarding timer expired while the client is getting authenticated with preferred method, but
intermediate auth state is successful.

•  auth_failed: Authentication failed

If the L2 authentication is failed, the reason code is updated with one of the following values. This value is
not populated only if the status is auth_failed, server-reject : RADIUS server rejected the client.

•  server-timeout : Timeout while connecting to RADIUS server

•  supplicant-timeout : The 802.1x client timed out

•  no-server: No authentication servers are configured

•  attribute-processing-error: An error occured when processing attributes received from the

authentication server.

L3 onboarding states and failure reason codes

L3 onboarding states include the following:

•  successful : DHCP exchange is successful

•  failed : Client failed to get IP through DHCP

•  timeout : Client did not get any response from the server

•  partial : DHCP exchange in one of the VLANs is not successful or in-progress

Failure Reason Codes

The reason code is updated when the status moves to 'failed'.

•  dhcp-failure : Client DHCP exchange failed

Public

Client Insight 557

•  no-dhcp : Client did not initiate DHCP exchange

NOTE

If a client is onboarding on multiple VLANs, consolidated onboarding status on all
the VLANs is considered to derive L3 onboarding status.

L4 onboarding states

•  dns : key denoting DNS on-boarding status value. Status values can be any of the following:

◦  successful : DNS exchange is successful.

◦  failed : Client failed to get the successful DNS response.

◦  timeout : Client did not initiate DNS request.

•  arp-to-gw: key denoting ARP to GW on-boarding status value. Status values can be any of the following:

◦  successful : ARP to GW exchange is successful

◦  failed : ARP to GW exchange failed, no response received from gateway

◦  timeout : ARP traffic not seen from client

Overall onboarding status and failure reason codes

•  successful : successful

•  failed : failed

•  timeout : Client did not get any response from the server

•  partial : Incomplete L2 and/or L3 onboarding

Failure Reason Codes: Reason code is updated when the status moves to failed.

•

l2_onboarding_failed : L2 onboarding failed

•

l3_onboarding_failed : L3 onboarding failed

•

l4_onboarding_failed : L4 onboarding failed

The following table depicts the overall client on-boarding status derivation based on L2, L3 and L4 stages.

Table 1. On‐boarding status derivation

L2 onboarding

L3 onboarding

L4 onboarding

Overall‐onboarding

Successful

Successful

Successful

Successful

Public

Client Insight 558

| Successful | Successful | Any state | L4 onboarding status |     |
| ---------- | ---------- | --------- | -------------------- | --- |
| Successful | Any state  | Any state | L3 onboarding status |     |
| Partial    | Successful | Any state | L2 onboarding status |     |
| Partial    | Partial    | Any state | Partial              |     |
| Partial    | Failed     | Any state | Failed               |     |
| Partial    | Timedout   | Any state | Timedout             |     |
Supported Platforms
Most client insight features, including data captures for L2 onboarding, L3 onboarding and L4 onboarding,
are supported on AOS-CX 4100i, 5420, 6000, 6100, 6200, 6300, 6400, 8100, 8325, 8360, 8400, and
10000 Switch Series. However, the client insight features that capture information about ARP latency and
ARP client failures to the gateway information are supported only on 6200v2, 6300 and 6400v2 Switch
series.
Prerequisites
Listed below are the prerequisites to configure client insight:
1.  DHCP snooping must be enabled to capture the L3 onboarding status of clients. If DHCP snooping is
not enabled, then all clients are marked with L3 timeout as onboarding status.
2.  Port access configuration is recommended if the authentication and latency values must be derived. If
port access is not configured, MAC learn is considered to be L2 onboarding success.
3.  For DNS onboarding latency, Traffic-Insight instance with monitor type dns-onboarding-latency must
be configured.
4.  For Average DNS Latency, Traffic-Insight instance with monitor type dns-average-latency must be
configured for DNS packet sampling.
5.  On platforms where the Traffic Insight feature is not available, the Average DNS Latency values are not
available.
6.  On platforms where Traffic-Insight and ARC features are not available, DNS on-board latency values are
not available.
Points to Note
•  Onboarding details will not be captured for clients onboarded during process restart.
•  There can be multiple onboarding event logs for a client if the client MAC ages out and rejoins
repeatedly.
|     | Public |     | Client Insight | 559 |
| --- | ------ | --- | -------------- | --- |

•  UBT clients are ignored by client insight.

•  Upon successful L3 onboarding, if switch relearns the client's MAC after the MAC ages out, L3

onboarding timestamp values would be older than L2 onboarding timestamps.

•

In case of unavailability of L2 and L3 timestamps, the timestamp value in the onboarding event log will
be displayed as  -1 .
Following are some of the scenarios where the L3 timestamp could be unavailable:

◦  For clients onboarded while client insight is in disabled state, followed by feature enable and client

MAC being aged out and relearnt in switch.
In this scenario, the event log generated will have L2 timestamps but not L3 timestamps.

◦  For successfully onboarded clients who have acquired IP address. Switch reboot will flush client MAC

from the system and any traffic other than DHCP exchange will trigger client onboarding.
In this scenario, L3 timestamp will not be available since the DHCP exchange did not take place
during client onboarding.

•  When a client is authenticated using port access device-mode, all client insight entries on the port

except device MAC address will be cleared from the database. Further, client insight will only monitor
device MAC client on the port and ignore other clients.

•

If a port is configured as a network port or is an LLDP-learned port, client insight will only monitor the
network infrastructure device on that port and ignore other clients.

•  Client insight will not monitor secured clients. Ongoing data will be updated for network infrastructure

only.

•  When LLDP is not configured or is disabled on the infrastructure device, the port and client will function

as normal.

•

If clients behind the switch are identified and updated as client insight, enabling LLDP will prevent
further updates for those clients if the MAC client is detected behind an infrustructure port.

Limitations

Listed below are the limitations for this feature:

•  Clients with static IP configuration—Static IP or static binding clients will enter L3 timeout state, as

DHCP process will not be triggered for them

•  L2 and L3 stage timeout values are configured as 3 minutes and 2 minutes. These values cannot be

modified.

•  For mac-auth clients, with parallel-auth/auth-priority, there will be delay in firing the on-boarding event.

This is because authentication status is published only after the 802.1x authentication process is
complete.

Public

Client Insight 560

•

If the client onboarding is successful after the onboarding timeout, no new event is generated. To
check the current onboarding status, HPE Aruba Networking Central must monitor the events from
appropriate features (port access, DHCP snooping). The same is applicable for clients where the
onboarding fails.

•  Old L3 latency values are published when the port to which clients are connected flaps, as the client will

not trigger DHCP process after L2 authentication on rejoin.

•  ND-snooping binding clients will enter L3 timeout state, as DHCP process will not be triggered for them.

•  Clients behind network infrastructure devices such as a switch will not be monitored by client insight,

regardless of whether they are dynamic or secured client

•  UBT clients will not be monitored.

•

If a port is configured as a network port or is an LLDP-learned port, Client Insight will monitor only the
network infrastructure device on that port, ignoring any clients behind those devices.

•  For secured clients, Client Insight will not monitor them, irrespective of whether the connected port is
configured in client mode or device mode. As a result Ongoing data will only be updated for network
infrastructure devices on network ports.

•  When LLDP is not configured or is disabled on the infrastructure device, the port will function as a

normal port, and clients behind the switch will be treated as regular clients.

•

If LLDP is enabled after the clients behind the switch have already been learned and updated as Client
Insight onboarded clients, those clients will no longer be updated for ongoing changes once it is noted
that the Client MAC is behind an infrastructure port.

•  DHCP static attributes such as Default Router, Server IP and Domain Name Servers are provided to

DHCP-Client by DHCP-Server as part of DHCP OFFER packet. These attributes are only supported for
DHCPv4 Clients and are not be available for the DHCPv6 Clients.

Secure client considerations

1.  For device-profile clients without security(dot1x/macauth) RADIUS-latency is not applicable.

2.  For Port-security client's RADIUS-latency and authentication_latency are not applicable.

3.  When a client is authenticated via device-mode, all Client-Insight entries on the port except device MAC
will be cleared from DB. Further, Client-Insight will only monitor device MAC client on the port and
ignore other clients.

4.  UBT clients will not be monitored by Client-Insight.

Authentication completion time calculation for some special cases is detailed below:

Case 1:

Public

Client Insight 561

1.  Ani nterface is configured as auth-priority dot1x mac-auth for a MacAuth client.

2.  The client state changed from Auth In-Progress to Interim-Auth Success (MacAuth)

3.  The interface configured with high priority auth-method failed (Dot1x)

4.  The client state changed from Interim-Auth Success(MacAuth) to Final-Auth Success (MacAuth)

5.  Auth_completion_time is updated in this point In this case client onboarded after Interim-

Auth(MacAuth) Success time, but auth_completion_time is updated after higher priority auth-
method(dot1x) failed. Hence, client authentication latency will be higher than the actual authentication
latency.

Case 2:

1.

Interface configured as auth-priority dot1x mac-auth for dot1x client

2.  Client state changed from Auth In-Progress to Interim-Auth Success(MacAuth).

3.

Interface configured high priority auth-method failed(Dot1x)

4.  Client state changed from Interim-Auth Success(MacAuth) to Final-Auth Success(MacAuth)

5.  Auth_completion_time is updated in this point

6.  Supplicant downloaded in background successfully and client moved Final-Auth Success(Dot1x). In this
case, client onboarded after dot1x client moved to Final-Auth Success, but auth_completion_time
value is updated once MacAuth client moved to Final-Auth Success. Therefore, client authentication
latency will be lesser than the actual authentication latency.

Feature Interoperability

•  DHCP snooping must be enabled for L3 onboarding stage updates.

•  A Traffic Insight instance must be created to check the average DNS latency values.

•  Authentication is not required for dynamic clients. Therefore, if port access is disabled on the port, it will
be considered as L2 successful soon after the MAC address is learnt. Client-Insight will print the event
log as Auth Successful for non-secured clients.

•  DHCPv6 snooping doesn’t support static-attributes option, hence DHCPv6 Server attributes will not be

printed in both client insight event log and in diag-dump output.

•  Port_name will be empty for Dynamic Clients in both OVSDB and event logs.

•  DHCPv4 and DHCPv6 onboarding events are displayed based on the Enablement of DHCPv4 or

DHCPv6 snooping.

Public

Client Insight 562

•  Once L3 onboarding is successful either when DHCPv4 or DHCPv6 complete, then any further attributes
exchanged are not captured in Client-Insight per VLAN onboarding columns. They would be updated in
the per VLAN ongoing columns.

The following table describes the features support across platforms that are required for publishing client-
insight information.

•  Port-Access : Provides detail about secure L2 onboarding.

•  DHCP-Snooping: Provides DHCP onboarding detail for the clients.

•  Traffic-Insight: Computes DNS latency and publishes it to client-insight table.
